sources: Next Step 05 Jun: 'Business case being built toward a Q4 2026 vendor decision'
Products__c: Agentic AI; ANA (analytics and agentic intake/negotiation scope implies procurement efficiency and spend metrics)
metrics.narrative: Business case is actively being built toward the Q4 2026 vendor decision per Next Step (05 Jun), indicating the buyer has defined a problem worth solving and is quantifying the case internally. However, no quantified business impact (cost savings, cycle-time reduction, error-rate improvement, headcount efficiency) has been stated or captured yet. The product scope (ANA + Agentic AI) implies analytics procurement spend visibility and AI-driven intake/negotiation automation, which typically anchor to procurement cycle time, maverick-spend reduction, or supplier-negotiation ROI—but the buyer has not articulated these metrics on record. The partial read: a business case is in motion (confirmed), the quantified value case is not yet externalized to Zycus (gap). Next move: during the upcoming integration meeting or case study presentation, co-develop the quantified savings/ROI model with the relevant stakeholder (Procurement Analytics/AI lead) and Rahul Chawla (Procurement Capabilities), tying ANA visibility and Agentic AI negotiation to their current pain (source: inferred from product scope, to be validated).

sources: OpportunityContactRole: Bestegul Iltir, Marcin Skarbon, Rahul Chawla (three Evaluators, active but no champion behaviors documented)
Avoma touchpoint 28 May: Rahul Chawla attended Agentic AI Roundtable (recent engagement 29d ago)
Next Step 05 Jun: 'Business case being built' (evaluators are engaged in case build, a positive signal)
Next Step 05 Jun: 'Effort focused on engaging and confirming decision makers and broadening contacts' (EB/DM access not yet achieved, champion would provide this)
champion.narrative: Champion is developing but not yet confirmed. The three named evaluators—Bestegul Iltir (Procurement Analytics Digital/AI and Insights Lead), Marcin Skarbon (Global Head of Talent Acquisition), and Rahul Chawla (Senior Manager, Procurement Capabilities and Programs)—are engaged and building the business case per Next Step (05 Jun), which is a positive signal. Rahul Chawla attended the Agentic AI Roundtable on 28 May (29 days ago), showing recent face-to-face engagement. However, none of these contacts has demonstrated champion behaviors yet: no evidence of internal advocacy (selling Zycus internally, pushing back on competitors, or driving the agenda), no access to the Economic Buyer or Decision Maker layer (those roles are still unmapped per Next Step), and no proactive sharing of internal intel (decision process, competitor positioning, or approval chain). They are active evaluators, not yet champions. The developing read: Rahul and Bestegul are engaged and building the case (recent activity, business-case workstream), but advocacy and EB access are not confirmed. What is missing: evidence that one of them is coaching us on how to win, introducing us upward, or differentiating Zycus vs iValua/Ariba internally. Next move: during the integration meeting or case study presentation, explicitly recruit Rahul or Bestegul as a champion—ask them how they see us vs the competition, what internal objections we should prepare for, and whether they can introduce us to the budget holder (CPO or VP Procurement). Make them a partner in the win, not just an evaluator.

sources: Competitors__c: iValua; SAP Ariba (both named, no additional detail)
Stage Qualified + Q4 2026 vendor decision (Next Step 05 Jun) implies active shortlist, neither competitor eliminated
Products__c: Agentic AI; ANA (our differentiation anchor vs analytics-first or ERP-suite rivals)
competition.narrative: Competition is confirmed and current. Two competitors are named in Competitors__c: iValua and SAP Ariba. Both are enterprise procurement platforms with broad S2P suites and established customer bases, making them credible peers in a shortlist for ANA+Agentic AI. No call transcript, Next Step detail, or task log provides additional color on their positioning, pricing, or buyer sentiment—so threat level and status are inferred from the field and the stage. At Qualified stage with a Q4 2026 vendor decision target and two named competitors, this is an active, live competitive shortlist; neither rival has been ruled out or faded. The wedge: Zycus differentiates on Agentic AI depth (Merlin negotiation agent, I2O intake automation) vs the analytics-first or ERP-suite positioning of iValua and Ariba. iValua is strong on spend analytics and sourcing orchestration but lighter on agentic AI; SAP Ariba is the ERP-integrated incumbent-style platform with broad adoption but less AI-native innovation. How we win: lead with the Merlin Agentic AI story (negotiation automation, intake intelligence) as the next-generation capability the buyer cannot get from iValua or Ariba, anchor it to their procurement-efficiency pain (to be quantified), and use the integration meeting to prove our API/ERP connectivity matches or beats Ariba's. Deliver a customer case study (planned per Next Step) showing Agentic AI ROI vs a traditional analytics-only approach, and position Zycus as the AI-first innovator vs the legacy-platform competitors.

sources: Products__c: Agentic AI; ANA (product scope implies procurement visibility and intake/negotiation pain, not yet stated by buyer)
Next Step 05 Jun: 'Integration concerns to be addressed' (system fragmentation or integration complexity is implied pain)

OpportunityContactRole: Bestegul Iltir (Procurement Analytics Digital/AI and Insights Lead), Rahul Chawla (Senior Manager - Procurement Capabilities and Programs) (roles imply pain ownership, no direct pain statement captured)
identify_pain.narrative: Pain is partially identified. The product scope (ANA + Agentic AI) implies two pain points: (1) lack of procurement spend visibility and analytics (addressed by ANA), and (2) manual, slow procurement intake and supplier negotiation (addressed by Agentic AI). Next Step (05 Jun) references 'integration concerns to be addressed,' suggesting current-system fragmentation or integration complexity is a friction point. However, no buyer has articulated the operational or financial pain in their own words—no quote naming cycle-time bottlenecks, maverick spend leakage, manual workload, or cost-out pressure. The three evaluators (Bestegul Iltir from Procurement Analytics/AI, Marcin Skarbon from the relevant stakeholder, Rahul Chawla from the relevant stakeholder) are the likely pain owners, but their specific pain statements have not been captured. The partial read: pain is inferred from product scope and integration concerns (confirmed indirectly), but the buyer has not externalized the pain severity, quantified the cost, or named the business trigger. Next move: during the upcoming integration meeting or case study presentation, ask Bestegul (Procurement Analytics/AI lead) and Rahul (Capabilities lead) to describe in their words what is broken today—how long intake takes, where spend visibility gaps hurt them, and what happens if they do nothing (the cost of inaction).

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are partially visible. Next Step (05 Jun) names 'a move to finalise contract terms' as a planned gate following the case study presentation, indicating that MSA/SOW negotiation is on the roadmap but not yet active. No detail on legal review, procurement channel (direct vs partner-led paper), InfoSec requirements, or redline expectations has been captured. The partner involvement (Versuni/Rahul Chawla co-contact-role) raises the question of whether this is a partner-transacted deal (paper flows through the reseller) or a direct Zycus contract with Versuni as an implementation/integration partner; Next Step notes 'partnership and pricing strategy still to be decided internally,' suggesting the commercial model is not yet locked. No current-contract expiration date or incumbent-replacement forcing function is on record. The partial read: contract finalization is planned (confirmed from Next Step), but the mechanics—legal/InfoSec gates, partner vs direct paper, procurement approval workflow—are unknown. Next move: during the pricing and partnership strategy discussion (noted as upcoming internal work), clarify with the deal team whether this is a direct or partner-transacted deal, and during the integration meeting with the buyer, confirm their procurement/legal process (approval levels, standard terms, InfoSec review required).

sources: Next Step 05 Jun: 'Effort focused on engaging and confirming decision makers' (gap acknowledged)

No Avoma call content referencing budget authority or commercial approval
economic_buyer.narrative: Economic Buyer is not yet identified. Next Step (05 Jun) explicitly names 'engaging and confirming decision makers' as an active workstream, acknowledging the gap. The three named contacts—Bestegul Iltir (Procurement Analytics Digital/AI and Insights Lead), Marcin Skarbon (Global Head of Talent Acquisition), and Rahul Chawla (Senior Manager, Procurement Capabilities and Programs)—are all evaluator-level roles; none holds budget authority at the C-level or senior VP procurement/finance layer. No sponsor or executive-level contact role is on record, and no call transcript references budget approval or commercial authority. For a $150k ANA+Agentic AI deal at an enterprise account, the EB likely sits in procurement leadership (CPO, VP Procurement) or finance (CFO approval chain). The inference: this is early qualification; EB mapping is the next gate, not yet reached. What the evidence shows: three active evaluators building the business case, but no upward thread to the budget holder. Next move: during the integration meeting or case study presentation, ask Bestegul or Rahul to name who will approve the budget and request an introduction to that sponsor, targeting the CPO or VP Procurement layer.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is partially mapped. Next Step (05 Jun) lays out a forward sequence: business case build (in progress) → integration meeting (upcoming) → case study presentation → contract-term finalization, with decision-maker confirmation and pricing strategy still underway. This implies a structured, multi-gate process: technical validation (integration), peer validation (case study), commercial negotiation (pricing/terms), and executive sign-off (DM/EB approval). The Q4 2026 vendor decision is the target milestone. However, the approval chain is not yet confirmed—Next Step notes 'engaging and confirming decision makers' is still active, meaning we do not yet know how many layers (procurement lead → CPO → CFO, or simpler) or whether a formal committee/RFP round is required. The three evaluators (Bestegul, Marcin, Rahul) are building the case but have not disclosed the final sign-off path. The partial read: we have the forward milestone sequence (integration → case study → contract → Q4 decision) but not the approval hierarchy or committee structure. Next move: during the integration meeting, ask Bestegul or Rahul to walk through the internal approval process—who reviews the business case, who signs off on the budget, and whether procurement, finance, or IT governance must bless it.

sources: Products__c: Agentic AI; ANA (product scope is a proxy for criteria—analytics + AI negotiation are weighted)
Next Step 05 Jun: 'Integration concerns to be addressed in an upcoming meeting' (integration feasibility is a criterion)

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are partially visible. The buyer has scoped the solution to ANA (analytics) and Agentic AI (intake/negotiation automation), signaling that procurement spend visibility and AI-driven negotiation are weighted criteria. Next Step (05 Jun) names 'integration concerns to be addressed in an upcoming meeting' and a 'case study presentation' as near-term gates, implying that system integration feasibility and peer-validation proof points are on the evaluation checklist. The competitive shortlist (iValua, SAP Ariba) suggests the buyer is benchmarking platform breadth, AI maturity, and enterprise integration capability. However, no formal RFP/RFI criteria, weighted scorecard, or use-case validation framework has been captured. What remains unknown: whether AI/Agentic capability is a differentiator or table-stakes, the relative weight of integration complexity vs feature depth, and any compliance/InfoSec gates. The partial read: product scope and integration are confirmed criteria (from Next Step and products), but the full decision framework and weighting are not yet externalized. Next move: during the integration meeting, request the formal evaluation criteria or BRD if one exists, and map our ANA+Agentic AI fit to their weighted priorities.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI fit signal is strong. Products (Agentic AI, ANA) are scoped, indicating the buyer has defined an AI-driven procurement automation and analytics requirement—this is not exploratory; the buyer is specifying AI as part of the solution. Rahul Chawla attended the Agentic AI Roundtable on 28 May (29 days ago), showing willingness to engage in AI-education sessions. No AIS score, status, or why-field is populated, so the formal AI-excitement rating is unknown. However, the product scope (Agentic AI explicitly named) and the early-stage AI event attendance are strong positive signals. The buyer is AI Curious at minimum, possibly AI Hungry (product scope suggests they see AI as a solution, not just a nice-to-have). No evidence of resistance or skepticism. The fit read: the buyer values AI and has scoped it into the evaluation; Zycus's Merlin Agentic AI (negotiation agent, intake automation) is well-aligned with their interest. Recommendation: at the integration meeting and case study presentation, anchor the Merlin story to quantified ROI (negotiation savings, intake cycle-time reduction) to move the buyer from Curious to Hungry (AI as a must-have differentiator, not just table-stakes).

Pulse: live, early-stage Qualified with healthy forward momentum toward Q4 2026 vendor decision. Partner-led motion (Versuni) means sparse Avoma coverage is structural, not a stall.

items.detail: Economic Buyer and Decision Maker are not yet identified (Next Step 05 Jun: 'engaging and confirming decision makers'). Three evaluators are engaged (Bestegul, Marcin, Rahul), but no EB/sponsor at the CPO or VP Procurement layer is mapped. For a $150k deal at an enterprise account, evaluators cannot close alone—budget authority sits higher. Without EB engagement before the case study presentation, we risk building a strong technical case that stalls at the approval layer or loses to a competitor with better executive sponsorship.

Integration concerns flagged as a near-term gate (Next Step 05 Jun: 'Integration concerns to be addressed in an upcoming meeting'). This is a standard enterprise evaluation checkpoint, not a red flag, but if not resolved cleanly, it becomes a blocker or a lever for SAP Ariba (whose ERP integration is a known strength). The buyer is testing whether Zycus can integrate with their current procurement/ERP stack. Must execute the integration discussion with confidence, clear API/connector roadmap, and proof points (customer examples of similar integrations) to neutralize this as a risk.

Pricing and partnership strategy are still being decided internally (Next Step 05 Jun: 'partnership and pricing strategy still to be decided internally'). This is a Zycus-side gap, not a buyer objection, but until resolved it blocks commercial progression. Unclear whether this is a direct Zycus deal or a partner-transacted deal through Versuni, which affects pricing model, margin, and contract mechanics. Internal alignment required before the buyer requests a quote or moves to negotiate terms.

Champion is developing but not confirmed. Rahul Chawla and Bestegul Iltir are engaged evaluators, but neither has demonstrated champion behaviors (internal advocacy, EB access, competitor-intel coaching). Without a champion who sells Zycus internally and introduces us to the EB, this deal stays evaluator-driven and vulnerable to a competitor with stronger internal sponsorship (e.g. if iValua or Ariba has a champion at the CPO layer, they can outmaneuver us even if our product is superior). Champion conversion is the highest-leverage political move.
items.first_raised: Inferred from OpportunityContactRole and Next Step 05 Jun

items.detail: Quantified business value / ROI metrics are not yet externalized. Business case is being built (Next Step 05 Jun), but the buyer has not stated a quantified savings target, cycle-time reduction goal, or cost-out mandate. Without a joint, quantified value case, the deal risks becoming a feature-comparison shootout where price and incumbent advantage (Ariba) or analytics depth (iValua) dominate. Co-developing the ROI model with Bestegul (Procurement Analytics/AI lead) and Rahul (Capabilities lead) at the integration meeting or case study presentation is critical to anchor the buyer's decision to measurable business outcomes that favor Zycus's AI differentiation.

champion_strength.source: OpportunityContactRole (Rahul Chawla, Bestegul Iltir as Evaluators), Avoma touchpoint 28 May (Rahul attended Agentic AI Roundtable), Next Step 05 Jun (both engaged in business case build)
champion_strength.summary: Champion is developing but not yet confirmed. Rahul Chawla (Senior Manager, Procurement Capabilities and Programs) is the most recently engaged evaluator—attended the Agentic AI Roundtable on 28 May (29 days ago) and is part of the business-case build workstream per Next Step (05 Jun). Bestegul Iltir (Procurement Analytics Digital/AI and Insights Lead) is also engaged in the business case. Both are positive signals (recent activity, evaluator-level buy-in), but neither has demonstrated champion behaviors: no evidence of internal advocacy (selling Zycus internally, pushing back on competitors, or driving the agenda), no access to the Economic Buyer or Decision Maker layer (those roles are still unmapped per Next Step: 'engaging and confirming decision makers'), and no proactive coaching (sharing competitor intel, approval-chain guidance, or internal objections). They are active evaluators, not yet champions. What is missing: evidence that one of them is a partner in the win, not just a participant in the evaluation. Without a champion who has EB access and advocates for Zycus internally, this deal stays evaluator-driven and vulnerable to a competitor with stronger internal sponsorship. Next move: during the integration meeting or case study presentation, explicitly recruit Rahul or Bestegul—ask them how they see us vs iValua/Ariba, what internal objections we should prepare for, and whether they can introduce us to the budget holder. Make them a co-architect of the win.

Without EB access, the technical case may stall at the evaluator layer or lose to a competitor with stronger executive alignment. Mapping the EB by early Jul positions Zycus to anchor the case study to the sponsor's priorities and secure sign-off before Q4 vendor decision.

items.trigger: Next Step 05 Jun: 'Integration concerns to be addressed in an upcoming meeting.' SAP Ariba is a named competitor (Competitors__c) whose ERP-integration strength is a known advantage; must prove Zycus API/connector capability matches or beats theirs.
items.expected_effect: Removes integration as a blocker or Ariba wedge. If the buyer perceives Zycus integration as risky or unproven, Ariba's ERP-native story wins by default. Clean execution of the integration discussion (customer examples, API/connector roadmap, proof of similar deployments) keeps Zycus in the shortlist and shifts the conversation to AI differentiation (our strength) vs platform breadth (Ariba's).

items.action: Anchor the case study presentation to quantified Agentic AI ROI vs iValua and Ariba.
items.trigger: Next Step 05 Jun: case study presentation planned. Competitors: iValua (analytics-first, lighter AI) and SAP Ariba (legacy platform, less AI-native innovation). Products: Agentic AI, ANA (our differentiation). Business case being built, but no quantified ROI externalized yet.
items.expected_effect: Lands the Merlin Agentic AI wedge (negotiation agent, I2O intake automation) as the next-generation capability neither iValua nor Ariba can match. A customer case study showing quantified savings (negotiation ROI, intake cycle-time reduction) vs an analytics-only (iValua) or legacy-platform (Ariba) approach frames the buyer's choice as legacy vs AI-first innovation. Without this, the deal risks commoditization and a price-driven shootout with iValua. Co-develop the quantified value case with Bestegul (Procurement Analytics/AI lead) and Rahul (Capabilities) during this presentation to anchor their internal business case to measurable Agentic AI outcomes.

items.expected_effect: Converts an evaluator into a partner in the win. A champion who sells Zycus internally, introduces us to the EB, and coaches on competitor positioning raises win probability more than any other single lever at this stage. Without a champion, this deal stays evaluator-driven and vulnerable to a competitor with stronger internal advocacy (e.g. if iValua or Ariba has a champion at the CPO layer, they can outmaneuver us even if our product is superior). During the integration meeting or case study presentation, ask Rahul or Bestegul: 'How do you see us vs iValua and Ariba? What internal objections should we prepare for? Can you introduce us to the person who will approve the budget?' Make them a co-architect of the win strategy.

items.expected_effect: Unblocks commercial progression and quote readiness before the buyer requests pricing or moves to negotiate terms. If the pricing model (direct vs partner-transacted, margin split, discount authority) is unresolved when the buyer asks for a quote, the deal stalls or the competitor with a ready commercial proposal wins by speed. Resolve internally by late Jul to stay ahead of the Q4 vendor decision timeline.

confidence_signals.summary: Deal is live and engaged. Last activity 05 Jun (21 days ago) with substantive Next Step update naming six active workstreams: business case build, integration meeting upcoming, case study presentation planned, contract-term finalization next, DM/EB mapping in progress, and pricing/partnership strategy being decided. Avoma touchpoint 28 May (Rahul Chawla attended Agentic AI Roundtable, 29 days ago). Three named evaluators engaged. No cooling signals: no forward slip, no buyer non-response, no long silence. The 21-day gap since last activity is well within normal pacing for a Qualified-stage deal in a 12-15 month enterprise cycle (188 days to close). Partner-led motion (Versuni) means sparse Avoma coverage is structural, not a stall. Confidence is Medium due to partial MEDDPICC coverage (EB/DM unmapped, champion developing, quantified metrics not externalized), but engagement is healthy and forward momentum is clear.

north_star_verdict.math: 188 days to close (31 Dec 2026). Created 29 May 2026, qualified same window (28d old). Time-in-Qualified: ~28 days. Last activity 05 Jun (21d ago) is well within the 12-15 month enterprise cycle and consistent with early qualification. No forward slip recorded. Next Step (updated 05 Jun) names Q4 2026 vendor decision, integration meeting upcoming, case study, and contract finalization sequence—credible pacing for a 6-month runway. For a $150k ANA+Agentic AI deal at Qualified with three evaluators named and two competitors shortlisted, 188 days is ample; the date is defensible.
evidence: Stage Qualified with 188d to close aligns with early enterprise motion (discovery, shortlist, business-case build) toward Q4 2026 vendor decision (Next Step 05 Jun).
Last activity 21 days ago (05 Jun) is recent for a Qualified-stage deal in a 12-15 month cycle; no stall.

Next Step log (05 Jun) is substantive and forward-looking: business case in progress, integration meeting upcoming, case study presentation planned, contract-term finalization next, DM/EB mapping and pricing strategy underway—six discrete workstreams, all active.
Three buyer evaluators from Procurement Analytics/AI, Talent Acquisition, and Procurement Capabilities on record; multi-threaded at evaluator layer.
Two named competitors (iValua, SAP Ariba) confirms live competitive shortlist, not sole-source.
Products (Agentic AI, ANA) scoped; $150k amount is credible for ANA+AI at this account size.
Partner-led motion (Versuni co-role) is noted in ground truth; sparse Avoma coverage is structural, not a stall signal.
One Avoma touchpoint 28 May (Agentic AI Roundtable, Amsterdam, Rahul Chawla attended) shows recent buyer engagement 29 days ago, consistent with early qualification.
No knowledge gaps that block the current stage; EB/DM/Champion mapping is the expected next gate for Qualified, not a deficit.
north_star_verdict.headline: Qualified-stage deal with 188 days to close, credible Q4 2026 vendor-decision timeline, but 21 days of radio silence since last activity (05 Jun). Two competitors active, no recent buyer engagement to confirm momentum or advance next steps.

best_practice_check.summary: Three high-leverage win-strategy levers for this deal: (1) Map and engage the Economic Buyer (CPO or VP Procurement layer) before the case study presentation—evaluators alone cannot close a $150k deal; leverage the integration meeting to request an EB introduction from Bestegul or Rahul. (2) Weaponize the Merlin Agentic AI story as the wedge vs iValua (analytics-first, lighter AI) and SAP Ariba (legacy ERP platform, less AI-native innovation)—the integration meeting and case study presentation are the two moments to land this differentiation with quantified ROI. (3) Convert one evaluator (Rahul Chawla or Bestegul Iltir) into an active champion who coaches us on internal objections, competitor positioning, and the approval chain—without a champion the deal stays evaluator-stalled and vulnerable to a competitor with better internal advocacy.

competitive_position.summary: Two active competitors on the shortlist: iValua and SAP Ariba. Both are credible enterprise procurement platforms; neither has been eliminated or faded as of the most recent activity (05 Jun, 21 days ago). No buyer sentiment, pricing feedback, or head-to-head positioning detail has been captured. Threat level is inferred from stage and product fit: iValua is the HIGHEST CURRENT THREAT—a direct S2P peer with strong spend analytics and sourcing orchestration, likely positioned as the analytics-first alternative to our ANA+Agentic AI story. SAP Ariba is the SECOND THREAT—a broad ERP-integrated platform with established enterprise adoption, but less AI-native innovation; likely positioned as the safe, incumbent-style choice. How we win: lead with Merlin Agentic AI (negotiation agent, intake automation) as the next-generation capability neither rival can match, anchor it to quantified procurement-efficiency ROI (to be co-developed), prove integration parity vs Ariba at the upcoming integration meeting, and deliver a customer case study showing Agentic AI value vs analytics-only (iValua) or legacy-platform (Ariba) approaches. Position Zycus as the AI-first innovator vs the legacy competitors.

competitors.name: iValua
competitors.quote: Named in Competitors__c; no additional buyer sentiment or positioning detail captured.
competitors.source: Competitors__c (last confirmed Next Step update 05 Jun)
competitors.how_we_win: Differentiate on Merlin Agentic AI depth (negotiation agent, I2O intake automation) vs iValua's analytics-first / sourcing-orchestration positioning. iValua is strong on spend analytics but lighter on agentic AI; lead with our AI-native innovation as the next-generation capability. Anchor to quantified procurement-efficiency ROI (cycle time, negotiation savings) and deliver a customer case study showing Agentic AI value vs a traditional analytics-only approach. Make the integration meeting a proof point that our API/ERP connectivity is enterprise-grade, not a blocker.

competitors.name: SAP Ariba
competitors.quote: Named in Competitors__c; no additional buyer sentiment or positioning detail captured.
competitors.source: Competitors__c (last confirmed Next Step update 05 Jun)
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as the AI-first innovator vs SAP Ariba's legacy ERP-integrated platform. Ariba has broad adoption and deep SAP/ERP integration (a strength if the buyer is an SAP shop), but less AI-native capability. Lead with Merlin Agentic AI as the procurement-transformation story Ariba cannot tell (negotiation automation, intake intelligence vs manual workflows). At the integration meeting, prove our API/ERP connectivity matches or beats Ariba's (neutralize their integration advantage), then use the case study presentation to show that Agentic AI ROI outpaces a traditional suite approach. Frame the choice: legacy platform (Ariba) vs next-generation AI (Zycus).

ai_positioning_strength.score: Strong product scope, under-positioned as the competitive wedge
ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is strong in product scope but not yet landed with force in the buyer conversation. Products (Agentic AI, ANA) are scoped, signaling the buyer values AI-driven procurement automation and analytics—this is Zycus's core differentiation vs iValua (analytics-first, lighter AI) and SAP Ariba (legacy platform, less AI-native innovation). The Agentic AI Roundtable on 28 May (Rahul Chawla attended, 29 days ago) shows early AI-education engagement. However, no evidence yet that the Merlin Agentic AI story (negotiation agent, I2O intake automation) has been positioned as the wedge vs the competitors, or that the buyer has articulated AI-driven ROI (negotiation savings, intake cycle time) as a decision criterion. The integration meeting (upcoming) and case study presentation (planned per Next Step 05 Jun) are the two moments to land the AI differentiation with quantified proof points. Current read: AI is scoped (strong), but not yet weaponized as the win wedge (under-positioned vs the competitive threat). Not critically under-positioned (the product scope is right), but the Merlin story must be the centerpiece of the integration and case study conversations to avoid commoditization.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Customer expectations are partially mapped. The buyer has scoped the solution to ANA (analytics) and Agentic AI (intake/negotiation automation), signaling alignment with Zycus's core strengths. Integration concerns are flagged as a near-term gate (Next Step 05 Jun), indicating the buyer is testing whether Zycus can integrate with their current systems (ERP, procurement stack)—this is a standard enterprise fit criterion, not a red flag, but must be addressed cleanly to stay aligned. The planned case study presentation suggests the buyer values peer validation and proof points, which Zycus can deliver. No exposures (misalignment on features, pricing, or deployment model) have surfaced. The fit read: product scope is strongly aligned (ANA + Agentic AI is our sweet spot), integration feasibility is the near-term test (upcoming meeting will resolve), and peer validation is valued (case study will satisfy). Proceed with confidence on product fit; execute the integration discussion cleanly to maintain alignment.

hard.next_step: Business case being built toward a Q4 2026 vendor decision. Integration concerns to be addressed in an upcoming meeting, followed by a case study presentation and a move to finalise contract terms. Effort focused on engaging and confirming decision makers and broadening contacts; partnership and pricing strategy still to be decided internally.

deltas.subject: Lead with Merlin Agentic AI as the wedge vs iValua and SAP Ariba. Both competitors are credible enterprise platforms: iValua is strong on spend analytics and sourcing orchestration (analytics-first positioning, lighter on agentic AI), and SAP Ariba is the broad ERP-integrated incumbent-style platform (established adoption, less AI-native innovation). Our differentiation: Merlin Agentic AI (negotiation agent, I2O intake automation) is the next-generation capability neither rival can match. High-leverage move: at the integration meeting (upcoming), prove our API/ERP connectivity matches or beats Ariba's (neutralize their integration advantage as a blocker perception), then at the case study presentation, deliver a customer story showing quantified Agentic AI ROI (negotiation savings, intake cycle-time reduction) vs an analytics-only (iValua) or legacy-platform (Ariba) approach. Frame the buyer's choice: legacy competitors vs AI-first innovation (Zycus). If the Agentic AI story does not land with force in these two moments, we risk commoditization and a price-driven shootout with iValua.

deltas.to: Convert an evaluator into an active champion. Rahul Chawla (attended Agentic AI Roundtable 28 May, 29 days ago) and Bestegul Iltir (Procurement Analytics/AI lea
deltas.subject: Convert an evaluator into an active champion. Rahul Chawla (attended Agentic AI Roundtable 28 May, 29 days ago) and Bestegul Iltir (Procurement Analytics/AI lead) are engaged and building the business case (Next Step 05 Jun), but neither has demonstrated champion behaviors yet—no evidence of internal advocacy (selling Zycus internally, pushing back on competitors), EB access (introducing us upward), or coaching (sharing competitor intel, approval-chain guidance). Without a champion, this deal stays evaluator-driven and vulnerable: a competitor with a strong internal advocate can outmaneuver us even if our product is superior. High-leverage move: during the integration meeting or case study presentation, explicitly recruit Rahul or Bestegul as a partner in the win. Ask them: 'How do you see us vs iValua and Ariba? What internal objections should we prepare for? Can you introduce us to the person who will approve the budget?' Make them a co-architect of the win strategy, not just an evaluator. A champion with EB access and internal advocacy raises win probability more than any other single lever at this stage.
